speaker: interviewer
text: --1, 1996. This is an interview with Leib, L-E-I-B, Dimerman, D-I-M-E-R-M-A-N, in Winnipeg, Manitoba, Canada. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] We were talking about your stay with the third front of the Ukrainian-Russian Army. And you felt that you were treated relatively well. How long did you stay with that particular battalion?

speaker: interviewee
text: With this battalion, I was six month. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We start to lose people. And, and we were involved in little battles and then started to shrink and shrink and shrink, the army. They sent us-- we're a formal point. We have-- in-- we-- they sent us, all of us, by-- the army was shrinking-- start small. We lost a couple of tank. We lost ammunition. We was-- we have to step out and step out, and the Germans coming up and up. And then they sent in Korosten-- in, in Groznyi. And Groznyi, this is North Caucasus. It was a point-- I don't know in English. I am-- they are forming-- they forming new armies over there. Now, they took out all of them. And they were forming an army. Instead, so many people, they're giving additional people. And they're helping-- they gave us new uniforms. So the uniforms were the wool. And who's healthy-- and they sent us back to fight.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they reorganized the--

speaker: interviewee
text: Reorganized--

speaker: interviewer
text: --formation of the--

speaker: interviewee
text: --absolutely.

speaker: interviewer
text: --battalions.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. It was in Groznyi. And then, I came back to the-- I came to the second Ukrainian front, which I was very scared maybe [LAUGHS] they will find out what happened.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were you afraid of at that point?

speaker: interviewee
text: Of when I ran away, when I lost the, the, the--

speaker: interviewer
text: Identity?

speaker: interviewee
text: Somebody stole my gun.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I was scared. It was in  the second Ukrainian front. Now, in time for war, it's no big-- not big connection between--

speaker: interviewer
text: Groups.

speaker: interviewee
text: --groups, not too much a connection. Is-- this is what my luck--

speaker: interviewee
text: Fear was.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. And they sent me to the second Ukrainian front. And they sent me in a tank division, which I was a soldier, a little boy, already older. I cleaned the tank. I washed the tank with a gun. I was behind the tank. Like I told you, the kids, they used. They took a couple of cows and cattle. They dressed us as shepherds. We came out to the highways. And we were watching. And the Germans were not suspicious. They figured it was local shepherds from the farms. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: And how would--

speaker: interviewer
text: --they treat you?

speaker: interviewee
text: And we gave information to the army.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hmm. How would the Germans treat you when they came across you as a shepherd?

speaker: interviewee
text: I tell you the truth, we didn't  come close even to them. I personally didn't come eye to eye. I didn't come close to them. When we are surveying, it can be around-- you know, they have binoculars. They see movement, cows, and young little kids. They didn't give attention.

speaker: interviewer
text: So now you are still with the 2nd Ukrainian Battalion.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what year would that have been? 1940--

speaker: interviewee
text: I was in the 44th Division-- [NON-ENGLISH] Division.

speaker: interviewee
text: No--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, this is 1944. I was injured. In 1943, end of '43, I was injured.

speaker: interviewer
text: While you were in that same battalion?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I was injured.

speaker: interviewer
text: How were you injured?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was injured just accidentally. They bombed the German. And the wave from the bomb-- something hit me. And I was injured here on my neck. And the teeth over here-- four-- you know, right away, threw it out. And they took me in hospital. And I was in hospital in Baku, Baku. This is Uzbekistan, as a-- is Republic of Russia. And after I came home out from the hospital in 1944, they sent me in the officer school.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long--

speaker: interviewee
text: And I was a cadet.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long were you in the hospital?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was in the hospital six months, maybe, yeah, six months, five and a half.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did they look after you in that hospital?

speaker: interviewee
text: Good, very good, with feeling, lots of mercy. We cannot-- this is-- was not complain.

speaker: interviewer
text: And that was a Ukrainian hospital? Where, where was--

speaker: interviewee
text: No--

speaker: interviewer
text: --this hospital?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, it was a hospital in Kavkaz. This is-- there are usually black people over there, a Respublik-- dark brown people. This is a, a Respublik. They're looking like Filipino people-- the-- that-- the skin type-- their religion Muslim.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this was now in the heart of Russia.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they nursed you back to health?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And after you were released from the hospital?

speaker: interviewee
text: Then they sent me to Groznyi. And in Groznyi was a military school academy. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I was studying in academy-- young officers-- a young lieutenant, which has graduated the youngest in my age. And I received one star, like a young lieutenant in tank knowledge, which is like tank mechanic. Now, usually in normal times, you have to spend at least two years to be a young lieutenant. Now, in time for war, they make it-- they cooked up very quickly personnel for the army. Very quickly, they prepared. And this was-- for a young lieutenant, it was six months.

speaker: interviewer
text: And after six month, they sent me back to the army. So you were being trained as a tank officer, as a cadet?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I was a lieutenant-- young lieutenant, where you have lieutenant. Then older lieutenant is three stars. And then it's going to be captain. This--

speaker: interviewer
text: And you were trained as a young lieutenant?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And after the six-month training, where did you go?

speaker: interviewee
text: After the six-month-- as a matter of fact, then I already have two medals. I was-- I received in the army. And when it's a young guy with two medals, they was treat-- they treat me very well.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you earned your medals for?

speaker: interviewee
text: For fighting--

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hmm.

speaker: interviewee
text: --bravery.

speaker: interviewer
text: What acts of bravery did they notice about you? What acts of bravery?

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't have a very high medals. Why, the medals were divided. They have in Russia very high medals, which I didn't have them. They have [NON-ENGLISH]. I didn't have them. And they have medals-- the Red Star, which was coming in three stages, which I didn't have them. They have normal just for fighting, bravery for fighting. And I was not a hero. But they have medals-- it's a gold star, like hero. I didn't have them. This kind of people, like, they have the special medals-- they treat them special, you know, with special respect.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you weren't in that category.

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't belong in this category. My medals is regular medals for bravery. One medal-- I have a good medal, strong, which, after the war, they paid money for the medals. Is-- they're very-- the very high medals, they used to-- people received 45, 50, 60 ruble a, a month. For my medal, the highest I received, 11.50.

speaker: interviewer
text: This was like a pension?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you now have been assigned two medals.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, six.

speaker: interviewer
text: Six medals.

speaker: interviewee
text: All together.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. And where were you--

speaker: interviewee
text: And I have two--

speaker: interviewer
text: --with the army at that point in 1944?

speaker: interviewee
text: In 1944, they sent me to fight. I was in front to Brest, Brest Litovsk. And we was fighting in Poland. We had gone to Poland to cut a road through to Poland. And we came to the Elbe-- in Germany, to the Elbe. We didn't cross the Elbe. We didn't make a pontoon. The bridge was broken. Now there-- the American Army came already from the other side of the Elbe, which the higher officers-- the high officers met each-- the other of the other side. It's--

speaker: interviewer
text: So the higher-- the higher officers met up with the American--

speaker: interviewee
text: The American--

speaker: interviewer
text: --officers?

speaker: interviewee
text: --officers, yeah, of the other side, which--

speaker: interviewer
text: And this was in Helba?

speaker: interviewee
text: Elbe. The, the, the, the lake is Elbe.

speaker: interviewer
text: Elbe, mm-hmm.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I was not on the other side. And then in 1945, the war was finished. And they let me go.

speaker: interviewer
text: So at the time that the war ended in 1945, you were in that area near the Lake-- River of Elbe?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, we came up to the Elbe. They have the [NON-ENGLISH], the Elbe, or the lake. They call this the Elbe. We came up, up to the Elbe. And I didn't go far deep to Germany. Why-- the American Army came from the other side in Germany and the English Army. And we came, and the French came. And we was from this side of the Elbe. And we were standing over there. We were staying over there. And when the war was finished in 1945, the Germans capitulated. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Then they, they don't need an army over there so much-- the old regular army over there. And I was young. And the older people, the very young people, they sent us back deep--

speaker: interviewer
text: They discharged you?

speaker: interviewee
text: --to Russia, yeah. And they asked me in case I wanted to stay in the army, to make a career in the army, which I refused. I was a little bit tired from the army. And I decided I want to be free to look for my parents.

speaker: interviewer
text: So where did you go when they discharged you?

speaker: interviewee
text: To tell you the truth, I didn't have too far to go. I didn't know where to go. I tried to come back to the town where I'm born.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which was Kostopol?

speaker: interviewee
text: Kostopol, which I didn't-- I didn't find anybody from relatives. I find out everybody was killed.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you went there.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, where they're killed. The grave they showed me, the local people. And the way they killed them-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] everything I find out. And I decide to leave the town.

speaker: interviewer
text: What had happened to your grandparents that you had become attached to?

speaker: interviewee
text: They killed them. The German killed them. They-- my grandmother, my grandfather-- their religion was very extreme religion people, Orthodox. And they were very Orthodox people. They took the rabbi, our rabbi. And they told the rabbi to make a meeting and to tell the people that death came with God send them. And everybody put the tallis, the people. First, they took the man in tallisim, tallisim. They put it in. And they took him-- we have, in forest a little bit out of town, a, a, a building they used to kill cattle over there. It was beside over there, they dig it out holes, graves, big graves. And, and they shot them over there. They shot them.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this was the end of your grandparents as well?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's how they died?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, over there.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you find out about that?

speaker: interviewee
text: When I came--

speaker: interviewer
text: Back?

speaker: interviewee
text: --I was talking to the local people. And some of them was nice people. And they told me what happened.

speaker: interviewer
text: How soon after you had left your grandparents did that tragedy happen to them? When did they die, in other words?

speaker: interviewee
text: After I left, I think, maybe around two weeks, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and the inquisition started.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you returned to Kostopol and found out this information. And your family was-- perished. Where did you go next?

speaker: interviewee
text: After I found out-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] next, you know, like I  told you, the army offered me to be in the army to make a career in the army. And they promised me to go to study more, which continued to study after the war to complete to be the same officers. One star, you have to continue to go another six months to spend it. And I decided I don't want to be a military person. And I didn't have a trade, didn't have a qualification. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I have a little bit money. And I was attending a concert in a circus. And I saw a magician was performing. And I would say, with God's help, I catched, you know, most-- a couple. And I start to repeat it into work. And I see I can make it. It's nothing complicated. And then I met another guy who was a performer. And I told him, I am very interesting. And I'm from the army. I have no qualifications, no trade, nothing. And he give me a couple secrets. They were old secrets, what is old for him. But it was new for me. Well, he's not using them. A couple books, so you put a little snake. And you take out flowers from the other side.

speaker: interviewer
text: These are magic acts?

speaker: interviewee
text: Magic act, yeah. And, and I came to a concert hall. And they had groups they used to travel performing. And I recommended myself-- I'm a performer. And they saw my tricks, and they like. And you need lots of guts not to be a performer, and not to ever dare even to go out on the stage. I never was on the stage.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you took a chance and--

speaker: interviewee
text: And I, I would say God help. And with God's help-- and I became a performer.

speaker: interviewer
text: A magician?

speaker: interviewee
text: A magician. And then I start to develop my own tricks, my own numbers. And I was working in the Moscow Philharmonic. I was working in Leningrad Philharmonic.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you develop these tricks?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. It just came.

speaker: interviewer
text: It just came to you?

speaker: interviewee
text: A fantasy was created. And I-- my fantasies, I make it to work. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I don't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: What about equipment that you needed to do these acts?

speaker: interviewee
text: Pardon me?

speaker: interviewer
text: The equipment that you needed to do these acts, where did you acquire them?

speaker: interviewee
text: The equipment is you make a schematic. And when you have a schematic, you came to have-- in the schematic, what you're making is connected with metal. You're going to a tinsmith. He will make you the little box. And you show him the way to make it. And according to the schematic, he's making this. And then you're developing your number-- the way to sell it, the product. It's the same thing like a salesman. And the way you have to introduce this new number to the public.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you developed--

speaker: interviewer
text: --your own acts.

speaker: interviewee
text: --I, I want to say I was a performer self-made. I was not educated in a school magician, which people didn't believe me after my performances. They figured if I finished-- didn't finish nothing. I did-- I saw one time in a film Houdinis with the chains, which I'm making this. I started to make Houdini's number with the chains. And, and they considered me a performer.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who did you attach yourself to when you performed, by yourself or with a group or--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I used-- we used to work with a group. I used to have an assistant to prepare for me everything for the numbers. And I used to be on the stage 20 minutes, 25 minutes, which is lots of time when it's different kind of performers-- acrobatic numbers and--

speaker: interviewer
text: So was it circus you were--

speaker: interviewee
text: I was with a--

speaker: interviewer
text: --attached with?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was with a circus. And then I was-- in Philharmonic, I was working.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which circus were you working with?

speaker: interviewee
text: Keo.

speaker: interviewer
text: Kiev?

speaker: interviewee
text: Keo.

speaker: interviewer
text: And was that a Russian circus?

speaker: interviewee
text: As a matter of fact, it's Jewish.

speaker: interviewer
text: How do you spell that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Keo, K-E-O, Keo.

speaker: interviewer
text: So it was a Jewish circus that traveled around?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: What cities did you [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] arrive at while you were doing-- while you were working with the circus?

speaker: interviewee
text: You see, in the beginning, I was assistant with them. And then I became independent by myself. And when I was working for myself, we-- like, like a variety group, singer, accordion, different kind of variety group. And I  have  my magic tricks. My performance was 20 minutes in a stage, which you always have better paid. The longer on you're on the stage, you get more paid. And we was a group of 18 people, 20 people.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your, your role was as a magician? Or did you do other things as well?

speaker: interviewee
text: I am a [RUSSIAN], too-- [RUSSIAN].

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you explain that?

speaker: interviewee
text: [RUSSIAN]. This is-- you're laying down. And you work with the feet. You're turning over--

speaker: interviewer
text: A balancing--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: --balancing--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, another person.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hmm. Like acrobatics?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, it's similar, a little bit connected. I was working with them. And then [RUSSIAN]-- I found out I'm not very talented. And I saw people much better from me. And I saw that I will never reach the talent what they have. And I decided to step out from this and just magic.

speaker: interviewer
text: So how long did you continue on as a magician?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was what I make a living in Russia, up the whole way-- up to the-- up to 19-- 1955-- up to 1954.

speaker: interviewer
text: So just to review, you worked as a magician. And you did a lot of travel with your, your performing group. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] after traveling through Europe, you finally arrive--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, you see, after the war, there was not too many-- the Russian didn't trust too many performers to go overseas. No. I was in Poland. But Poland, Poland was communistic. I was in Bucharest, in Romania. It was communist. They let us out for concerts. You know, like, in the capitalist countries, no, I didn't travel.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then you arrived in Siberia. How did that happen?

speaker: interviewee
text: You see, after I was-- something I say, I was involved with against the Russian law. And it was a court-- witnesses-- what I say. And this-- and what I say what was fighting, who was wrong, was not worded.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, you said something that offended the--

speaker: interviewee
text: Anyway, I don't want to repeat it. It's not important. You see, the point is they gave me 25 years.

speaker: interviewer
text: For what you said?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. And they sent me to Siberia.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year was that you got that--

speaker: interviewee
text: This is in--

speaker: interviewer
text: --prison?

speaker: interviewer
text: So you said something that offended the political system.

speaker: interviewee
text: Or 1952, or something. I can't-- I can't remember. I have-- a matter of fact, I have a paper. They let me out from jail.

speaker: interviewer
text: So then you were sent to Siberia.

speaker: interviewee
text: I sent to--

speaker: interviewer
text: This was a--

speaker: interviewee
text: --Siberia.

speaker: interviewer
text: --prison term.

speaker: interviewee
text: I was sent to Arkhangelsk, where the people-- where they have a lot of years, like, 25 years, 20 years. They sent it deeper to Siberia, more security places. Then they sent-- they sent me to Kolyma, Kolyma. This is a place in Russia, in Siberia, and six month is daylight, and six month is dark. You don't see-- you don't know when is day and when is-- the six month is dark. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: So this was your prison term--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: --for what you had said.

speaker: interviewee
text: And six month, you can, in the middle of the night, read a book outside. You don't need light. This is-- was Kolyma. I was over there.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your prison term in Arkhangelsk, Siberia, like?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was-- in the, the beginning, they sent me over there to cut wood. It was very hard work. The first three months were very hard, big snows, cold. And I never was used to, to physical work. And I used to cut the big trees-- big work. And I never did enough work to have better food. I always was behind work, which food-- I received less like somebody else. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you survive--

speaker: interviewee
text: And--

speaker: interviewer
text: --that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Then they find out I'm a performer. And they put a hand in me. And they give me a light job, a librarian, in jail-- in camp. And I prepared concerts for the holidays in the camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Arkhangelsk?

speaker: interviewee
text: Pardon me?

speaker: interviewer
text: Was that in Arkhangelsk?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was in Kolyma, Kolyma. Kolyma, this is, a matter of fact, not far from Alaska from the other side.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why were you transferred to that second location?

speaker: interviewee
text: By-- the first location, Arkhangelsk, was just a, a point--

speaker: interviewer
text: An arrival--

speaker: interviewee
text: --where they--

speaker: interviewer
text: --center?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. The people were coming in from every-- each direction. Over there, they are sorting up. And when they see 25 years, in 5 years, I, I don't have to-- I have no right to live close to a big city. In 10 years, I don't have the right to vote. It's practically, like, 40 years they give me. This kind of-- type of people-- they don't want to read exactly details. I'm not a dangerous person. No, they put in right away signature, uh-huh, Kolyma, Kolyma, Kolyma. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: So they transferred you there after.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's right. they figured up I am extreme dangerous for their society. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: That you were dangerous, or you weren't?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, they figure up-- they figure up-- what kind of dangerous I am?

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm.

speaker: interviewee
text: I never was a dangerous person. I never kill nobody.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the name of the new place you arrived at was--

speaker: interviewee
text: Pardon me?

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the name of the new location they sent you to, Kalyngu? Near Alaska.

speaker: interviewee
text: The-- Kolyma.

speaker: interviewer
text: Kolyma. And what [SIGHS] were you expected to do there?

speaker: interviewee
text: What-- like I told you, over there was-- the first three months was tough. I was working [SIGHS] in bush [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] very hard. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] a new climate for me and the food and everything take together-- a different kind of personality people. I didn't like it. And you have to live with them under one roof. And this all-- that's the environment around me. I wasn't used to this-- be a performer, actor in a different society, before a officer. And right now, if you're showing around on the underworld, different kind of people, different kind of background.

speaker: interviewer
text: So it was pretty harsh.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, yes, very harsh. And, like, when they put me up later, I was the editor in the local paper. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And I improved my situation. I was the librarian. And I'm-- I forming concerts. And they was happy they had a person to organize the concerts. And when it was coming Russian holidays, they're making concerts in the camps over there. And after this, I felt much better. I have good food-- I mean, good food-- I was not hungry. And I didn't work hard. I have the opportunity to learn myself and to lift up a little bit my education, what I couldn't catch up.
